item.title: 6-DOF Robot Development
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Project Overview

This project designs a 6-DOF (Degree of Freedom) fully-actuated underwater robot (AUV) for underwater grasping tasks and high-performance motion control. Compared with traditional under-actuated or over-actuated AUVs, the 6-DOF fully-actuated scheme can achieve higher-level motion control capability on a compact design basis, completing complex underwater operation tasks. Additionally, to simultaneously satisfy high-precision underwater grasping and high-performance motion control, this paper designs two motion modes for the AUV: grasping mode and cruising mode. The grasping mode uses traditional control schemes to improve precision, while the cruising mode uses DRL-driven control to achieve complex motion decoupling.

1.1 Design Objectives

  • High-performance mechanical structure design
  • Traditional control scheme design
  • DRL control scheme design
  • Target recognition and grasping
  • Autonomous path navigation

2. Mechanical Design

2.1 Thruster Distribution Scheme

Traditional thruster distribution is divided into three categories: under-actuated, fully-actuated, and over-actuated.

Type Characteristics Application
Under-actuated Fewer thrusters than DOFs Simple observation tasks
Fully-actuated Equal thrusters and DOFs Precise operation tasks
Over-actuated More thrusters than DOFs High redundancy tasks

Considering that the robot needs to achieve high-level grasping tasks on a compact design basis, this design adopts the 6-DOF fully-actuated scheme.

The conventional fully-actuated scheme uses 6 fixed thrusters or 4 vector thrusters. Although vector thrusters have better maneuverability, they have complex structure and low reliability. Therefore, this design proposes a 4-thruster + 2-servo hybrid propulsion scheme:

  • 4 main thrusters provide primary thrust
  • 2 servos control the mechanical arm angle

2.2 Mechanical Structure

The robot body adopts a frame-type structure, mainly consisting of:

Robot System
├── Main Frame
│ ├── Sealed Cabin (Electronic Equipment)
│ ├── Battery Compartment
│ └── Buoyancy Material
├── Propulsion System
│ ├── Main Thrusters × 4
│ └── Servos × 2
├── Robotic Arm
│ ├── Shoulder Joint
│ ├── Elbow Joint
│ └── Gripper
└── Sensor System
 ├── Depth Sensor
 ├── Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)
 └── Camera

2.3 Material Selection

  • Main Body: Aluminum Alloy (lightweight and corrosion-resistant)
  • Seals: O-rings (Fluororubber)
  • Fasteners: Stainless Steel (seawater corrosion-resistant)
  • Buoyancy Material: EPP Foam

3. Control Scheme

3.1 Kinematics Modeling

Establish the robot coordinate system and use DH parameters for kinematics modeling:

Joint $\theta$ $d$ $a$ $\alpha$
1 $\theta_1$ $d_1$ 0 $90°$
2 $\theta_2$ 0 $a_2$ 0
3 $\theta_3$ 0 $a_3$ 0
4 $\theta_4$ 0 0 $90°$
5 $\theta_5$ 0 0 $-90°$
6 $\theta_6$ $d_6$ 0 0

Forward kinematics equation: $$T_i^{i-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

3.2 Traditional Control Scheme

For grasping mode, PID controller is used for low-level motion control:

class PIDController {
public:
 double kp, ki, kd;
 double setpoint, current_value;
 double integral = 0;
 double last_error = 0;
 
 double compute(double feedback) {
 double error = setpoint - feedback;
 integral += error;
 double derivative = error - last_error;
 last_error = error;
 return kp * error + ki * integral + kd * derivative;
 }
};

PID controller parameter tuning:

  • Position control: $K_p = 1.5$, $K_i = 0.2$, $K_d = 0.5$
  • Attitude control: $K_p = 2.0$, $K_i = 0.3$, $K_d = 0.8$

3.3 Deep Reinforcement Learning Based Control Strategy

To achieve complex grasping tasks, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) is used for control strategy training.

3.3.1 Network Architecture

Using Actor-Critic architecture:

  • Actor Network: Policy network $\pi(a|s)$
  • Critic Network: Value network $V(s)$

3.3.2 State Space

$$s = [p_{pos}, p_{vel}, q, \dot{q}, target_{pos}, image]$$

Where: position, velocity, joint angle, target position, image

3.3.3 Reward Function

$$r = r_{target} + r_{smooth} + r_{collision}$$

  • $r_{target}$: Target achievement reward
  • $r_{smooth}$: Motion smoothness reward
  • $r_{collision}$: Collision penalty

3.3.4 Training Algorithm

Using PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) algorithm for training:

class RobotAgent:
 def __init__(self):
 self.actor = ActorNetwork()
 self.critic = CriticNetwork()
 
 def select_action(self, state):
 mean, std = self.actor(state)
 action = torch.distributions.Normal(mean, std).sample()
 return action
 
 def update(self, trajectories):
 # Compute advantage function
 advantages = compute_gae(trajectories)
 # Update policy
 self.actor.optimize(trajectories, advantages)
 # Update value function
 self.critic.optimize(trajectories)

4. Vision System

4.1 Object Detection

Using YOLOv8 for underwater object detection:

model = YOLOv8('yolov8n.pt')
results = model.predict(image, classes=['target_object'])

4.2 Depth Estimation

Using monocular depth estimation network:

depth_model = MiDaS()
depth_map = depth_model.predict(image)

4.3 Grasp Point Detection

Using GraspNet for grasp point prediction:

grasp_poses = graspnet.predict(point_cloud)

5. Simulation Results

5.1 Kinematics Simulation

Kinematics verification in Gazebo:

Metric Simulation Theoretical Error
Maximum Depth 50m 50m 0
Maximum Speed 1.5 m/s 1.6 m/s 6.25%
Positioning Accuracy ±5cm - -

5.2 Control Strategy Training

DRL training curve shows:

  • After 500 episodes, success rate reaches 85%
  • After 1000 episodes, success rate reaches 95%

5.3 Physical Testing

Underwater experiment results show:

  • Robot can achieve independent control of 6 degrees of freedom
  • Grasping success rate reaches over 90%
  • Endurance time is about 2 hours

6. Conclusion

This paper designs a 6-DOF fully-actuated underwater robot. Main contributions include:

  1. Propulsion Scheme Innovation: Proposed 4-thruster + 2-servo hybrid propulsion scheme
  2. Control Strategy Optimization: Introduced deep reinforcement learning for autonomous grasping
  3. Compact Structure Design: Achieved fully-actuated configuration in limited space

This robot can be applied in underwater exploration, cultural relic salvage, marine biological research, and other fields.

6.1 Improvement Directions

  • Further optimize robotic arm end accuracy
  • Enhance vision algorithm robustness in turbid water environment
  • Extend underwater endurance time

item.title: The 20th National Undergraduate Intelligent Vehicle Competition (Full Model Group)
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Competition Overview

The National Undergraduate Intelligent Vehicle Competition is one of the college student science and technology competitions endorsed by the Ministry of Education. It aims to cultivate students’ innovative practical abilities and teamwork spirit. The 20th competition includes multiple groups such as the Full Model Group, Vision Group, and Hovercraft Group. The Full Model Group requires participating teams to design and build intelligent vehicles capable of autonomously completing track recognition, element processing, and path planning tasks.

This project is based on the EdgeBoard embedded platform, using YOLOv3-Mobilenet object detection algorithm combined with traditional image processing methods to implement a complete intelligent vehicle control system.

2. Hardware Platform

2.1 Main Control Platform

This project uses Baidu EdgeBoard as the main control platform with the following specifications:

Parameter Specification
CPU ARM Cortex-A53/A72
GPU Mali-T860
Computing Power 3.0 TOPS
Memory 4GB LPDDR4
System Ubuntu 18.04

EdgeBoard has a built-in PaddlePaddle inference engine, supporting deployment of various deep learning models, and has strong real-time image processing capabilities, making it very suitable for intelligent vehicle competition applications.

2.2 Sensor Configuration

  • Camera: Industrial camera for track image acquisition
  • Serial Port: USB-to-Serial converter connecting main controller and motor driver board
  • Battery: 12V lithium battery providing power for the entire vehicle

3. Software Architecture

3.1 System Architecture

The system uses modular design with the following core modules:

SmartCar
├── driver/ # Hardware Driver Layer
│ ├── camera_driver.h # Camera driver
│ └── uart.hpp # Serial communication
├── vision/ # Vision Processing Layer
│ ├── ImageCapture.h # Image capture
│ └── ImageProcess.h # Image processing
├── detection/ # AI Detection Layer
│ └── detection.hpp # YOLOv3 object detection
├── element/ # Element Recognition Layer
│ ├── Findline.h # Path extraction
│ ├── RingElement.h # Ring processing
│ └── CrossElement.h # Intersection processing
└── control/ # Motion Control Layer
 └── Control.h # PID control and speed planning

3.2 Core Code Structure

The main program entry is in src/icar.cpp, and the core class SmartCar (defined in src/SmartCar.hpp) coordinates all modules:

class SmartCar : public SingletonHolder<SmartCar> {
public:
 ImageCapture imagecapture; // Image capture
 std::shared_ptr<Detection> detection; // AI detection
 Findline findline; // Path planning
 Control control; // Motion control
 std::shared_ptr<Uart> uart; // Serial communication
 
 // Track element processing
 std::unique_ptr<Ring> ring;
 std::unique_ptr<Cross> cross;
 std::unique_ptr<Bridge> bridge;
 std::unique_ptr<Restaurant> restaurant;
 // ...
};

4. Key Technology Implementation

4.1 Object Detection

The YOLOv3-Mobilenet model is used for track element detection, supporting recognition of the following elements:

  • Charging Area (BATTERY)
  • Black Box Obstacle (BLOCK)
  • Bridge (BRIDGE)
  • Burger Obstacle (BURGER)
  • Traffic Cone (CONE)
  • Crosswalk (CROSSWALK)
  • Pedestrian (PEDESTRIAN)
  • Dashed Line (LINE)

Model inference uses multi-threaded processing to ensure real-time detection and image capture:

void SmartCar::ai_process_mul() {
 auto ai_process_task{
 [](std::shared_ptr<Detection> detection){
 cv::Mat m;
 while(smartcar.ai_runable){
 if(!smartcar.ai_picture_update.load()) continue;
 m = std::move(smartcar.image_for_predict);
 detection->inference(m); // Model inference
 smartcar.predict_result = detection->results;
 smartcar.ai_picture_update = false;
 }
 }
 };
 predict_thread = std::jthread(ai_process_task, this->detection);
}

4.2 Track Element Processing

The system supports recognition and processing of various track elements:

Ring Processing

The ring is a challenging element in intelligent vehicle competitions. The system implements ring entry and exit using a combination of AI detection and image processing:

else if(scene == Scene::RingScene){ 
 control.motorSpeed = ring_params->ring_speed; 
 control.turnP = CarParams->ring_p;
 control.turnD = CarParams->ring_d;
 findline.line_type = LineType::STRAIGHT;
}

Charging Area Processing

The charging area requires completing three phases: parking, charging, and exiting:

if (scene == Scene::ChargeScene ){
 if(charge->flag_down){
 control.motorSpeed = ai_params->charge_speedHigh;
 }
 if(charge->step == Charge::Step::Enter){
 control.motorSpeed = std::max(ai_params->charge_speedLow - ((float)charge->enter_count/(float)50), (float)1);
 }
 if(charge->carStoping){
 control.motorSpeed = 0; // Stop for charging
 }
}

4.3 Motion Control

Motion control uses PID controller combined with dynamic look-ahead strategy:

void SmartCar::motion_control() {
 // Dynamic look-ahead adjustment
 if(CarParams->qianzhan_change && control.motorSpeed <= CarParams->speedHigh && control.motorSpeed >= CarParams->speedLow){
 control.qianzhan = CarParams::qianzhan_low + 
 static_cast<int>(static_cast<float>(CarParams->speedHigh - control.motorSpeed) / 
 static_cast<float>(CarParams->speedHigh - CarParams->speedLow)) * 
 (CarParams->qianzhan_high - CarParams->qianzhan_low);
 }
 
 // Send control commands via serial port
 uart->carControl(control.motorSpeed, control.servoPwm);
}

4.4 Serial Communication

Serial communication is used for data exchange between the main controller and motor driver board:

bool SmartCar::uart_connect() {
 uart = std::make_shared<Uart>("/dev/ttyUSB0");
 int ret = uart->open();
 if (ret != 0) {
 std::cerr << "Uart Open failed!" << std::endl;
 return false;
 }
 uart->startReceive();
 return true;
}

5. Compilation and Running

5.1 Environment Configuration

Since the code uses C++20 features, gcc 11.2 needs to be installed:

# Compile and install gcc 11.2
cd ~/Downloads
wget https://mirrors.nju.edu.cn/gnu/gcc/gcc-11.2.0/gcc-11.2.0.tar.xz
tar -xf gcc-11.2.0.tar.xz
cd gcc-11.2.0
./contrib/download_prerequisites
mkdir -p gcc-11.2.0-build && cd gcc-11.2.0-build
../gcc-11.2.0/configure --prefix=/opt/gcc-11.2 --enable-languages=c,c++ --disable-multilib
make -j"$(nproc)"
sudo make install

# Configure environment variables
echo 'export PATH=/opt/gcc-11.2/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

5.2 Building the Project 

cd ~/car-work
rm -rf build && mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. 
make icar -j3

5.3 Running the Program 

./icar

6. Summary

This project implemented an intelligent vehicle control system based on EdgeBoard, completing the following work:

  1. Hardware Integration: Completed hardware connection and debugging between EdgeBoard and sensors/actuators
  2. Software Architecture: Designed and implemented a modular software architecture for easy maintenance and extension
  3. Object Detection: Deployed YOLOv3-Mobilenet model for multi-element detection
  4. Motion Control: Implemented path tracking algorithm combining PID control and dynamic look-ahead
  5. Element Processing: Completed recognition and processing of various elements including ring, intersection, bridge, and charging area

This project won the Second Prize in the South China Division of the 20th National Undergraduate Intelligent Vehicle Competition Full Model Group.

item.title: 2024 National Undergraduate Mathematical Modeling Contest (Problem A)
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Problem Background

“Bench Dragon” is a traditional folk activity in eastern Zhejiang Province. It consists of multiple wooden benches connected together, with lanterns fixed on each bench. During the performance, the dragon head leads the entire body to move. Since the benches are connected through pivots, complex spatial motion is generated during movement. This problem requires establishing a mathematical model to analyze the motion patterns of the Bench Dragon and perform optimization design.

1.1 Problem Description

Given a Bench Dragon composed of N benches, each bench has length L and width W. Benches are connected through pivots. The dragon head moves along a given trajectory. The requirements are:

  1. Determine the pose (position and orientation) of each bench
  2. Calculate the minimum turning radius
  3. Optimize speed planning

2. Model Assumptions

To simplify the problem, we make the following assumptions:

  1. Rigid Body Assumption: Each bench is treated as a rigid body, ignoring elastic deformation
  2. Connection Constraint: Adjacent benches are connected through pivots, which act as spherical joint constraints
  3. No-Slip Assumption: Each point of the dragon body moves along the tangential direction of the trajectory without lateral sliding
  4. Constant Speed Assumption: The dragon head moves at a constant speed V along the trajectory

3. Mathematical Modeling

3.1 Coordinate System Establishment

Establish a follower coordinate system: taking the dragon head as the reference, the tangential direction of the trajectory is the x-axis, upward is the z-axis, and right is the y-axis.

Let the trajectory parametric equation of the dragon head be: $$\vec{r}(s) = (x(s), y(s), z(s))$$

Where s is the arc length parameter.

3.2 Kinematics Model

For the i-th bench (i=1 represents the first bench after the dragon head), its position can be obtained by the following recursive relationship:

$$\vec{r}_{i+1} = \vec{r}_i + L \cdot \vec{t}(s_i) + \vec{d}_i$$

Where:

  • $\vec{t}(s)$ is the trajectory tangent vector
  • $\vec{d}_i$ is the offset vector due to pivot clearance

The orientation is calculated using the Frenet-Serret framework:

$$\vec{t} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{ds}, \quad \vec{n} = \frac{d\vec{t}}{ds} / |\frac{d\vec{t}}{ds}|, \quad \vec{b} = \vec{t} \times \vec{n}$$

3.3 Constraint Conditions

3.3.1 Geometric Constraints

The angle between adjacent benches must satisfy: $$\theta_{i,i+1} \leq \theta_{max}$$

Where $\theta_{max}$ is determined by the pivot’s range of motion.

3.3.2 Motion Constraints

Velocity constraint: $$v_i = v_{i-1} \cos(\theta_{i,i+1})$$

Angular velocity constraint: $$\omega_i \leq \omega_{max}$$

3.4 Minimum Turning Radius Solution

The minimum turning radius can be solved through the following optimization problem:

$$\min R$$ $$s.t. \quad \theta_{i,i+1} \leq \theta_{max}, \quad \forall i$$

Numerical iteration method is used with the following algorithm flow:

  1. Initialize trajectory curvature $\kappa = 1/R$
  2. Iteratively update curvature until constraints are satisfied
  3. Output the minimum feasible radius R

4. Optimization Design

4.1 Speed Planning

Based on kinematic constraints, establish a speed optimization model:

$$\min T = \int_0^L \frac{ds}{v(s)}$$ $$s.t. \quad a_{max} \geq |\vec{a}(t)|, \quad v_{max} \geq |\vec{v}(t)|$$

Trapezoidal speed curve is used for planning:

v(s) = 
 v_0 + (v_max - v_0) * s / s_1, s < s_1
 v_max, s_1 ≤ s < L - s_1
 v_max - (v_max - v_0) * (s - L + s_1) / s_1, s ≥ L - s_1

4.2 Bench Spacing Optimization

Considering the performance effect and motion flexibility, bench spacing can be optimized as:

$$d_i = d_0 + k \cdot \kappa(s_i)$$

Where $\kappa$ is the trajectory curvature and k is the adjustment coefficient.

5. Simulation Verification

MATLAB simulation is performed with the following parameter settings:

Parameter Value
Number of Benches N 33
Bench Length L 1.2m
Maximum Pivot Angle $\theta_{max}$ 30°
Dragon Head Speed V 1m/s

Simulation results show:

  1. Minimum feasible turning radius is approximately 2.5m
  2. Optimized speed curve can reduce motion time by 30%
  3. Dynamic spacing strategy can effectively reduce stress concentration

6. Conclusion

This paper establishes a kinematics model of the Bench Dragon, solves the minimum turning radius through constraint analysis, and proposes speed planning and spacing optimization schemes. The model can provide theoretical guidance for actual dragon dance performances.

6.1 Innovations

  1. Established a recursive pose model based on the Frenet framework
  2. Proposed a curvature-based adaptive spacing strategy
  3. Implemented a constraint-based speed optimization algorithm

6.2 Limitations

  1. Randomness of pivot clearance not considered
  2. Dynamic wind resistance not considered
  3. Model assumptions are overly idealized

Future research can introduce probabilistic constraints and dynamics models for improvement.

item.title: Spline Curves Summary
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Introduction

Spline curves are essential tools in computer graphics, robot path planning, and curve fitting. Compared to high-degree polynomials, spline curves offer better local control and numerical stability, ensuring smooth curves while reducing computational complexity.

2. Spline Curve Fundamentals

2.1 Definition

A spline curve is a smooth curve composed of polynomial segments, where each segment between two knots can be represented by a polynomial. Given a knot sequence $t_0 < t_1 < … < t_n$, a k-th degree spline curve satisfies:

  • It is a k-th degree polynomial on each interval $[t_i, t_{i+1}]$
  • It has continuous (k-1)-th derivatives throughout the entire domain

2.2 Advantages

  1. Locality: Modifying one control point only affects the local region
  2. Stability: Low-degree polynomials have better numerical stability
  3. Flexibility: Curve shape can be adjusted by changing control points
  4. Smoothness: Automatic continuity of derivatives at connection points

3. Common Spline Types

3.1 B-Spline

B-Spline (Basis Spline) is the most commonly used spline curve, with basis functions defined as:

$$B_{i,p}(t) = \frac{t - t_i}{t_{i+p} - t_i} B_{i,p-1}(t) + \frac{t_{i+p+1} - t}{t_{i+p+1} - t_{i+1}} B_{i+1,p-1}(t)$$

Where p is the spline degree.

The B-spline curve is calculated as: $$C(t) = \sum_{i=0}^n N_{i,p}(t) P_i$$

Where $N_{i,p}(t)$ is the p-th degree B-spline basis function, and $P_i$ are control points.

Properties:

  • Convex Hull: The curve lies within the convex hull of the control polygon
  • Locality: The i-th control point only affects the interval $[t_i, t_{i+p+1}]$
  • Variation Reducing: The number of intersections with any line does not exceed that with the control polygon

3.2 NURBS

NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline) is the standard curve representation in industry:

$$C(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^n R_{i,p}(t) w_i P_i}{\sum_{i=0}^n R_{i,p}(t) w_i}$$

Where the rational basis function is: $$R_{i,p}(t) = \frac{N_{i,p}(t) w_i}{\sum_{j=0}^n N_{j,p}(t) w_j}$$

Advantages:

  • Supports precise conic curve representation
  • Curve shape can be adjusted through weights
  • Unified representation of free-form curves and analytical curves

3.3 Catmull-Rom Spline

Catmull-Rom spline is an interpolating spline that passes through all control points:

$$P(t) = [t^3 \quad t^2 \quad t \quad 1] M P$$

Where $M$ is the Catmull-Rom matrix.

Characteristics:

  • Interpolation passes through all control points
  • Tangents are automatically calculated: $C’(t_i) = \frac{P_{i+1} - P_{i-1}}{2}$

3.4 Bézier Curve

Bézier curve is a special form of B-spline, defined by Bernstein basis functions:

$$B(t) = \sum_{i=0}^n B_{i,n}(t) P_i$$

Where the Bernstein basis function is: $$B_{i,n}(t) = \binom{n}{i} t^i (1-t)^{n-i}$$

4. Path Planning Applications

4.1 Robot Trajectory Generation

In robot path planning, spline curves are used to generate smooth motion trajectories:

// Cubic B-spline trajectory planning
class SplineTrajectory {
public:
 std::vector<Eigen::Vector3d> control_points;
 std::vector<double> times;
 
 Eigen::Vector3d position(double t) {
 // Calculate position on B-spline curve
 return evaluateSpline(t);
 }
 
 Eigen::Vector3d velocity(double t) {
 // Calculate velocity (first derivative)
 return evaluateDerivative(t, 1);
 }
 
 Eigen::Vector3d acceleration(double t) {
 // Calculate acceleration (second derivative)
 return evaluateDerivative(t, 2);
 }
};

4.2 Time-Optimal Planning

Combined with velocity/acceleration constraints, perform time-optimal planning:

$$\min T$$ $$s.t. \quad |v(t)| \leq v_{max}, \quad |a(t)| \leq a_{max}$$

Trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profiles are used for planning.

4.3 Continuity Requirements

Robot motion requires $G^2$ continuity (position, velocity, acceleration continuous):

Continuity Level Requirement
$G^0$ Position continuous
$G^1$ Tangent continuous
$G^2$ Curvature continuous

5. Numerical Computation

5.1 Parameterization Methods

Curve parameterization affects curve shape:

  1. Uniform Parameterization: $t_i = i / n$
  2. Chord Length Parameterization: $t_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} |P_j - P_{j-1}| / L$
  3. Centripetal Parameterization: $t_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \sqrt{|P_j - P_{j-1}|} / L$

5.2 Curve Differentiation

The derivative of a spline curve can be obtained by differentiating the basis functions:

For B-splines, the derivative of a p-th degree spline is still a (p-1)-th degree B-spline: $$\frac{d}{dt}C(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} N_{i,p-1}(t) \cdot \frac{p}{t_{i+p+1} - t_i}(P_{i+1} - P_i)$$

5.3 Curvature Calculation

Curve curvature is defined as: $$\kappa(t) = \frac{|x’y’’ - y’x’’|}{(x’^2 + y’^2)^{3/2}}$$

6. Simulation Example

Spline curve simulation using MATLAB:

% B-spline curve plotting
control_points = [0 0; 1 2; 2 1; 3 3; 4 0];
t = 0:0.01:1;
points = zeros(length(t), 2);

for i = 1:length(t)
 points(i,:) = bspval(t(i), control_points);
end

plot(control_points(:,1), control_points(:,2), 'o-');
hold on;
plot(points(:,1), points(:,2), 'r-');

7. Summary

Spline curves are core tools for robot path planning. This article summarizes:

  1. Theoretical Foundation: Mathematical definition and properties of spline curves
  2. Common Types: B-spline, NURBS, Catmull-Rom, Bézier
  3. Engineering Applications: Trajectory generation and time-optimal planning
  4. Numerical Methods: Parameterization and derivative computation

Choosing the appropriate spline type depends on the specific application:

  • Trajectory Interpolation: Catmull-Rom preferred
  • Free-form Curves: B-spline or NURBS
  • Precise Representation: NURBS (conic curves)

7.1 Further Reading

  • De Boor, C. - A Practical Guide to Splines
  • Piegl, L. - The NURBS Book
  • Choi, J. - Robot Path Planning

item.title: CF-DWTA Model Derivation: Damage Probability Model Considering Cascading Effects
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Research Background

In modern warfare systems, attacking key targets often requires considering cascading damage effects. When a critical node is destroyed, it may cause functional degradation or failure of other connected nodes. This phenomenon is called “Cascading Damage.” For example, destroying a communication base station may cause nearby radar stations to lose information support, thereby affecting the overall effectiveness of the air defense system.

Traditional damage probability models only consider the direct damage effect of a single strike, ignoring the relationships between targets. Therefore, establishing a damage probability model considering cascading effects has important theoretical significance and practical value.

2. Problem Definition

There are N interconnected target nodes, forming a network system $G=(V,E)$, where:

  • $V = {v_1, v_2, …, v_N}$ represents the set of nodes
  • $E \subseteq V \times V$ represents the set of edges, indicating the relationships between nodes

2.1 Basic Assumptions

  1. Each node $v_i$ has an initial functional value $F_i \in [0, 1]$, where 1 means fully functional and 0 means completely failed
  2. Each node has a failure threshold $\theta$; when the functional value falls below the threshold, it is considered failed
  3. Edge $(v_i, v_j)$ has a weight $w_{ij} \in [0, 1]$, indicating the degree of dependency between nodes

3. Mathematical Model

3.1 Direct Damage Model

Assume that after striking target $v_i$, its functional value decreases to: $$F_i’ = F_i \cdot (1 - d_i)$$

Where $d_i$ is the damage degree to the target, $d_i \in [0, 1]$.

The damage degree $d_i$ follows a probability distribution, usually described by exponential or normal distribution: $$P(d_i \leq x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$ or $$P(d_i \leq x) = \Phi\left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

3.2 Cascading Damage Model

Considering the dependency between nodes, define the functional value of node $v_i$ as: $$F_i = f({F_j}{j \in N(i)}, {w{ij}})$$

Where $N(i)$ is the set of neighbor nodes of $v_i$.

Using the weighted average model: $$F_i = \sum_{j \in N(i)} w_{ij} \cdot F_j$$

When a node is struck and its functional value decreases, it affects neighbor nodes through the above relationship, generating cascading effects.

3.3 Damage Probability Calculation

Define the system damage probability as the probability that system functionality drops below the threshold: $$P_{damage} = P\left(\sum_{i=1}^N F_i < \theta \cdot \sum_{i=1}^N F_i^0\right)$$

Where $F_i^0$ is the initial functional value of the node.

3.4 DWTA Model

The DWTA (Damage Weapons Target Assignment) problem can be expressed as:

$$\max \sum_{i=1}^N P_i(x_i)$$ $$s.t. \sum_{i=1}^N c_i x_i \leq C$$ $$x_i \in {0, 1}$$

Where:

  • $P_i(x_i)$ is the damage probability of node i after strike
  • $c_i$ is the resource consumption required to strike node i
  • $C$ is the total resource constraint

4. Model Solution

4.1 Monte Carlo Simulation

Due to the nonlinear characteristics of cascading effects, analytical solutions are difficult to obtain. Monte Carlo method is used for simulation:

Algorithm: Monte Carlo Simulation
Input: Network G, strike plan S
Output: Damage probability estimate P_damage

for k = 1 to M:
 for each node i in V:
 if i in S:
 d_i = Random(distribution)
 F_i = F_i^0 * (1 - d_i)
 else:
 F_i = F_i^0
 
 repeat:
 changed = false
 for each node i in V:
 F_i_new = sum(w_ij * F_j for j in N(i))
 if |F_i_new - F_i| > epsilon:
 F_i = F_i_new
 changed = true
 until not changed
 
 if sum(F_i) < theta * sum(F_i^0):
 count++
 
P_damage = count / M

4.2 Approximate Analytical Solution

For large-scale networks, mean-field approximation can be used:

Assuming node functional values follow a normal distribution $F_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$, then the system functional value: $$\sum_{i=1}^N F_i \sim N\left(\sum_{i=1}^N \mu_i, \sum_{i=1}^N \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{(i,j)\in E} \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j\right)$$

Where $\rho_{ij}$ is the correlation coefficient between nodes.

5. Simulation Analysis

5.1 Parameter Settings

Parameter Value
Network Size 50 nodes
Average Degree 4
Dependency Weight 0.3
Damage Threshold 0.4
Simulation Times 10000

5.2 Result Analysis

The simulation results show:

  1. Cascading effects can increase total damage probability by approximately 20-40%
  2. Network robustness is closely related to node degree distribution
  3. Identification of critical nodes has significant impact on damage efficiency

6. Conclusion

This paper establishes a damage probability model considering cascading effects. The main contributions include:

  1. Proposed a cascading damage propagation mechanism based on functional dependency
  2. Provided a Monte Carlo calculation method for damage probability
  3. Analyzed the influence of network topology on damage effects

This model can provide decision support for weapons target assignment and can also be used to evaluate the anti-damage capability of key targets.

6.1 Application Scenarios

  • Combat Command: Selecting optimal target combinations
  • Defense Evaluation: Evaluating anti-damage capability of critical infrastructure
  • Plan Formulation: Developing targeted defense strategies

6.2 Limitations

  1. Dependency assumptions are oversimplified
  2. Time dynamics are not considered
  3. Model parameters are difficult to obtain accurately

item.title: Second training session for SmartCar – connect the edgeboard
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
1. Power On the Smart Car

First, check if the battery voltage is between 11.6~12.6V—avoid overcharging or over-discharging. Then, insert the battery into the car and turn on the power switch. Finally, wait for the EdgeBoard to show a green light.

2. Connect to Computer

2.1 Wired Connection (First Connection)

  1. Use an Ethernet cable to connect the computer to the smart car
  2. Open the terminal and enter the command (may need multiple attempts if it fails):
ssh edgeboard@paddlepi

Enter the password: edgeboard to connect successfully. Note: If you cannot connect at this step, try multiple times. If it still doesn’t work, contact me to replace the board.

2.2 Wireless Connection (Most Common)

Wireless connection is commonly used when both the paddle and your computer are connected to the same hotspot. For the first wireless connection, enter the following in the terminal:

sudo nmtui

Click “active connect” to connect to your hotspot. After that, to connect to the car, simply open your hotspot, wait for the car to connect, then enter in the terminal:

ssh edgeboard@paddlepi

Password: edgeboard

3. Connect to VSCode via SSH

3.1 Install Plugin

First, install Remote - SSH in VSCode—this is very easy.

3.2 Connect to EdgeBoard via Plugin

  1. Click the “+” button in ssh-remote
  2. Enter in the input field:
ssh edgeboard@paddlepi
Password: edgeboard
  1. Click “Allow” all the way through. For the configuration file, select the first option (remember this configuration file location)
  2. Open the car-work folder in VSCode:
/home/edgeboard/car-work

This step will fail 90% of the time. Because EdgeBoard is very old and uses Ubuntu 18.04, you need to lower your VSCode version and then reconnect to the board. Through testing, 1.85.2 is the latest version that can connect to Ubuntu 18.04. Download link: https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_85

3.3 Configure SSH Key (Optional)

If you don’t want to enter a password every time you SSH into the board, you can configure an SSH key so you don’t need to enter the password each time. Steps:

  1. Enter the command in Windows terminal: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com" Note: Change your_email@example.com to something you recognize. I personally prefer naming like long753-win
  2. Copy the content of the public key (the one with .pub suffix) and paste it into edgeboard’s .ssh/authorized_keys
  3. Enter in Windows’ ./ssh/config file, in the same format as in my video: IdentityFile C:\Users\cl284\.ssh/id_ed25519

4. Open VNC Graphical Interface

If you haven’t installed VNC yet, open the URL https://www.realvnc.com/en/connect/download/viewer/, install the Windows version of the VNC client, and follow the installation instructions.

After logging into the EdgeBoard terminal, enter the command:

vncserver :1 -localhost no

Here, :1 represents the graphical interface port number, which can be set as needed. Prioritize using port 1. When occupied, try other ports—only use ports 1~4.

When you see the following message, it indicates successful startup:

New 'paddlepi:1 (edgeboard)' desktop at :1 on machine paddlepi

Starting applications specified in /etc/X11/Xvnc-session
Log file is /home/edgeboard/.vnc/paddlepi:1.log

Use xtigervncviewer -SecurityTypes VncAuth,TLSVnc -passwd /home/edgeboard/.vnc/passwd paddlepi:1 to connect to the VNC server.

Then open VNC and establish a new connection (if you’ve connected before, just click the one you created):

vncserver: paddlepi:5901
name: edgeboard

Also, if you’re not using port 1, for example port 2, the corresponding address should be: vncserver: paddlepi:5901

5. Configure Computing Board Environment

Since the code uses C++20 features and some other dependencies, we need to update the dependencies first.

5.1 g++11.2 gcc11.2 (Please reserve an afternoon for this installation!!!!)

The original repository is no longer available, so we use wget to fetch it:

# Fix environment
sudo apt --fix-broken install -y
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y build-essential xz-utils bison flex wget

# Download source code
cd /home/edgeboard/Download
wget https://mirrors.nju.edu.cn/gnu/gcc/gcc-11.2.0/gcc-11.2.0.tar.xz
sha256sum gcc-11.2.0.tar.xz
tar -xf gcc-11.2.0.tar.xz

# Download dependencies and compile
cd gcc-11.2.0
./contrib/download_prerequisites
cd ..
mkdir -p gcc-11.2.0-build && cd gcc-11.2.0-build
../gcc-11.2.0/configure \
 --prefix=/opt/gcc-11.2 \
 --enable-languages=c,c++ \
 --disable-multilib \
 --enable-bootstrap
make -j"$(nproc)" # This takes a long time, approximately 1.5 hours, please reserve enough time; subsequent steps will be faster
sudo make install

# Modify PATH
echo 'export PATH=/opt/gcc-11.2/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/opt/gcc-11.2/lib64:${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify
which gcc
gcc --version
g++ --version

5.2 libavdevice-dev 

sudo apt-get install libavdevice-dev

6. Build the Project

In the VNC interface, open the terminal, navigate to the project directory, and enter the commands:

 cd /home/edgeboard/car-work # Navigate to your workspace
 rm -rf build
 mkdir build
 cd build
 cmake .. # If you modified the entire code framework, you may need to modify the CMakeList file—ask AI how to do this
 make icar -j3

After complete configuration, to build after saving your modified code, enter the command in the build directory:

 make icar -j3

Here, “icar” is the target file to compile, and “-j3” means use three threads for compilation to speed up the build time. The build time varies depending on your changes.

7. Run the Program

In the terminal, navigate to the project directory and enter:

 ./icar

At this point, the car starts successfully. To stop the car’s movement, simply enter Ctrl+C in the terminal.

Troubleshooting

1. When logging into VNC, it requires root password, but entering edgeboard doesn’t work. 

cd /root/.vnc
touch xstartup
vim xtartup

Copy the following into it:

unset SESSION_MANAGER # Uncomment this line
exec /usr/bin/startxfce4 # Uncomment this line
#exec /etc/X11/xinit/xinitrc # Uncomment this line

[ -x /etc/vnc/xstartup ] && exec /etc/vnc/xstartup
[ -r $HOME/.Xresources ] && xrdb $HOME/.Xresources
xsetroot -solid grey
vncconfig -iconic &
xterm -geometry 80x24+10+10 -ls -title "$VNCDESKTOP Desktop" &
gnome-session gnome # Add this line to use GNOME desktop environment
twm &

Restart VNC:

vncserver -kill :1
sudo vncserver :1 -localhost no

2. EdgeBoard environment is corrupted and needs system reflash

Open Baidu Cloud Drive: Files shared via cloud: 赛事专用卡-镜像开源资料 Link: https://pan.baidu.com/s/1j32EjYg4vMcMBbzc1kMviA?pwd=1111 Extraction code: 1111 Flash the EMMC image, and follow the “EdgeBoard赛事专用卡-镜像烧写说明” (EdgeBoard Competition Card Image Flashing Instructions) in the compressed file

item.title: First training session for SmartCar – basic Git and Linux operations
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
This document covers only the fundamental aspects of Git and Linux systems, which should be sufficient for most scenarios encountered in the Smart Car Competition. Beyond these basics, there are many advanced topics not covered here. Based on my experience, these advanced features are less frequently used in the competition, so I recommend learning them only when you encounter a specific need.

Furthermore, there is no need to study Git and Linux “systematically” from scratch; doing so will consume a significant amount of your time with minimal return for the competition itself. I encourage everyone to learn through practice and application rather than blindly pursuing theoretical mastery. Given my own limitations, please be understanding if there are any errors or inaccuracies in this guide!

1. Git Essentials

1.1 Why Use Git?

Git is a widely-used distributed version control system, typically used in conjunction with GitHub or Gitee (Windows users can download it directly from the official Git website).

  • Remote Repositories: Create and manage codebases with GitHub/Gitee (Crucial).
  • Version Control: Track and manage different versions of your code.
  • Collaboration: Enable multiple team members to work on the same project.

Using GitHub or Gitee to create a repository and regularly back up your code is vital! This prevents code loss in case the car’s hardware fails unexpectedly. You should commit your code every time you finish a tuning session.

Recommended Workflow: Create local repositories on both the car’s onboard computer and team members’ laptops, all linked to a single remote repository. Push code from the car and Pull code to the laptops to avoid merge conflicts.

1.2 GitHub or Gitee?

Since our embedded boards (such as EdgeBoard or RK3588) usually connect via mobile hotspots, accessing GitHub directly can be unreliable without a proxy. For simplicity, I recommend using Gitee for code backups. The operations for Gitee and GitHub are nearly identical, so the skills learned here apply to both. Advanced users can still use GitHub via proxies or by setting up mirror repositories between the two platforms.

1.3 Creating a Remote Repository on Gitee

  1. Log in to Gitee.
  2. Click Create Repository, set a name, and set it to Private.
  3. Go to Management -> Repository Member Management -> Administrator -> Invite User. Add your teammates’ accounts and set their permissions to Manager.
  4. Now, you and your teammates can collaborate on this remote repository!

1.4 Creating a Local Repository

Create an empty folder in a directory (avoid Chinese characters in the path), open your terminal in that folder, and run:

git init

You now have an empty local Git repository.

1.5 Linking Local and Remote Repositories 

git remote add origin [https://gitee.com/yourname/yourworkSpace.git](https://gitee.com/yourname/yourworkSpace.git)

Verify the link using git: remote -v

1.6 Common Git Commands ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

To submit your first piece of code:

touch README.md
git add .
git commit -m "Your version info/message"
git push origin master

To pull code:

git clone [https://gitee.com/yourname/yourworkSpace.git](https://gitee.com/yourname/yourworkSpace.git)
git pull origin master

Force Push (Use only if you are certain your local version is the absolute latest!):git push -f origin master

Check current file status: git status

1.6.2 Code Version Control

View commit history: git log --oneline

Revert to a specific version: git reset (hash value)

1.6.3 Remote Repository Management

View the current remote repository: git remote -v

Add a remote repository: git remote add origin https://gitee.com/xxx/xxx.git

1.6.4 Branch Management

Branch management is a very important feature of Git, but I don’t recommend it for beginners. Firstly, branch management involves a significant amount of Git knowledge, and mastering git branch requires further study. Secondly, the intelligent vehicle competition project isn’t large, and collaboration among team members is relatively easy; there’s no need to overcomplicate things. However, I still suggest learning git branch, as it’s quite commonly used in large projects.

View branches: git branch Create branch: git branch branch_name Switch branch: git switch branch_name Merge branch: git merge branch_name Delete branch: git branch -d branch_name

2. Linux Basics

2.1 Linux in a Smart Car?

The default system for the board is Linux. We need to be proficient in using various Linux commands for file management, dependency acquisition, system configuration, etc. Here we will introduce some commonly used Linux commands during vehicle setup. Of course, the content of the Linux system is far more extensive than this; students can learn more during practical use.

Unlike Windows, most Linux operations are performed using the terminal. Therefore, we need to learn some commonly used bash commands.

2.2 Changing Directories

Enter a specified directory: cd /path/dir

Return to the user’s home directory: cd ~

Go back to the previous directory: cd ..

2.3 File Management

List all files in the current directory: ll, ls

Create a file: touch file.txt

Create a folder: mkdir dir

Delete a file: rm file.txt

Delete a folder: rm -rf dir/

Copy a file (source and index): cp a.txt b.txt

Copy a directory (source and index): cp -r dir1 dir2

Rename (source and index): mv a.txt b.txt

Move: mv a.txt dir

2.4 Vim Usage

Vim is a powerful tool that allows you to edit code directly in the terminal. Although code can be edited in VS Code, it’s still advisable to learn how to use Vim just in case.

Vim has three modes: Normal, Insert, and Command

  • Normal mode: This is the default mode when opening a file with Vim. In this mode, you can only view the code and use keyboard shortcuts to manipulate it.

  • Insert mode: Use i to switch from Normal mode to Insert mode, and use ESC to switch from Insert mode to Normal mode. In this mode, we can modify code.

  • Command mode: Enter : to enter, then enter certain commands to save and exit the file.

Related commands:

  • Modify file: vim file.txt

  • Save and exit: :wq

  • Force exit without saving: :q!

  • Force save and exit: :wq!

  • Learn other commands as needed.

2.5 Permission Management*

This section is only used when encountering a few unusual issues.

View permissions: ll

Modify permissions: chmod a+x file.txt (where r is read permission, w is write permission, and x is execute permission)

2.6 Other

Connect to hotspot: sudo nmtui

3 Resources & Assignments

AI Teachers: ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, Claude, Copilot, Deepseek (Random Sorting)

Git Tutorial: https://liaoxuefeng.com/books/git/introduction/index.html

Assignments:

  1. Each group should create a remote repository.

  2. The student responsible for writing the code should practice Git operations.

  3. The student responsible for writing the code should practice Linux operations. You can use a virtual machine to play around with it, or you can practice after we receive the board.

item.title: Linux Configuration Issues
item.author: cl2848077709@outlook.com (Chen Long)
item.description:
一、Common Commands

Check camera devices: ls -l /dev/video* Start Network Manager: sudo systemctl start NetworkManager

二、Common Errors

1. Issues potentially caused by graphics drivers:

If any of the following issues occur, configure the graphics driver first:

  • Screen flickering
  • Dual-monitor resolution issues

2. Unable to adjust screen brightness

Use xrandr to force adjust screen brightness:

# 1. Identify your monitor ID
xrandr | grep " connected" # Assuming the output starts with DP-4

# 2. Adjust brightness to 0.6 (range 0.0-1.0)
xrandr --output DP-4 --brightness 0.6

3. WiFi icon disappeared

The WiFi icon disappears because Ubuntu, while executing sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade, enables the automatic kernel upgrade service (unattended-upgrades) by default. This causes the kernel to upgrade from 5.15.0-67-generic to 5.15.0-130-generic. Since the new kernel is incompatible with the AX211 network card driver, the driver fails to load, firmware files cannot be loaded correctly, and the kernel module versions do not match, ultimately causing the wireless card to malfunction and the WiFi icon to vanish.

You can use the uname -r command to check the current kernel version. If it differs from the version when the WiFi was working, it is likely that the automatic system update caused a kernel upgrade that is incompatible with the AX211 driver.

  1. Roll back to the previous kernel version
    After rebooting, select “Advanced options for Ubuntu” at the boot interface to enter the GRUB menu. Then, select the original kernel version (5.15.0-67-generic) to boot and test if the WiFi functionality is restored.

  2. Lock the current kernel version

sudo apt-mark hold linux-image-5.15.0-67-generic
sudo apt-mark hold linux-headers-5.15.0-67-generic
sudo apt-mark hold linux-modules-5.15.0-67-generic
sudo apt-mark hold linux-modules-extra-5.15.0-67-generic
  1. Verification Enterapt-mark showholdto check if the lock was successful
  2. Modify GRUB configuration sudo nano /etc/default/grub

三、Common Configurations

1. Latex Configuration

Local installation command:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install texlive-latex-extra texlive-lang-chinese texlive-science latexmk texlive-xetex -y

For VS Code, simply sync your GitHub configuration.

2. FishROS one-click configuration 

wget http://fishros.com/install -O fishros && . fishros

3. .bash_aliases Configuration 

# 代理配置
alias proxy_on='export http_proxy="http://127.0.0.1:7897"; export https_proxy="http://127.0.0.1:7897"; export all_proxy="socks5://127.0.0.1:7897"'
alias proxy_off='unset http_proxy; unset https_proxy; unset all_proxy'

# 快捷传送
alias cdgs='cd ~/projects/GS-MOPSO'
alias cdd='cd ~/.dotfiles/'

# 常用指令
alias ...='cd ~'
alias gpu='watch -n 1 nvidia-smi'
alias sb='source ~/.bashrc'
alias v='vim'
alias ga='git add'
alias gc='git commit -m'
alias gp='git push'
alias gs='git status'
alias br='xrandr --output DP-4 --brightness' # 调整显示亮度

4. Double-click icon to minimize

Configure: gsettings set org.gnome.shell.extensions.dash-to-dock click-action 'minimize' Restore: gsettings reset org.gnome.shell.extensions.dash-to-dock click-action

四、Common Operations

1. Reallocating Linux disk space

  1. Enter the Live System (Third System):
    • Create an Ubuntu bootable USB drive (standard installation media).
    • Press F9 to enter the BIOS, boot from the USB drive, and enter the Live environment.
  2. Perform disk space operations.
  3. Verify if the disk space meets requirements.
  4. Appendix: Reference Video
